pattern: Since every operation can only raise a value and never lower one, the cheapest way to make a run non-decreasing is to raise every element up to the highest value seen so far in that run, so tracking a running maximum while sliding the window's start forward turns counting qualifying subarrays into a single pass with an early cutoff.
hints: For a fixed left end of a subarray, walk rightward while keeping track of the highest value seen so far in the window; any element smaller than that running maximum needs enough operations to raise it up to that maximum, and no other element ever needs to move.

The running total of needed operations for a fixed left end never decreases as the window grows to the right, so the moment it exceeds k you can stop extending that window entirely -- every further extension would only cost more.
Every single-element subarray always needs zero operations and is automatically valid, so start each left end's count at one before extending rightward.

edge_inputs.description: strictly decreasing with no operation budget
zero operation budget still allows the single-element subarrays
operation budget large enough to fix the whole array
one steep drop early on that only a generous budget can repair
repeated values need no operations at all
a small dip followed by recovery, testing that the running maximum persists across the dip

stress.description: random arrays with a random operation budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 2000)))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    return [nums, k]

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case, much faster typically
